mnemonics: DICE: Defaults Implement Concrete Extensions. Think of adding new features (extensions) to an interface without breaking existing implementations, as easily as rolling a DICE.
keyPoints: Default methods allow you to add new functionality to interfaces without forcing existing classes to implement it.
They are defined using the `default` keyword and provide a method body.
A primary motivation was to enable API evolution, like adding new methods to the `Collection` interface without breaking all code that implements it.
Classes that implement the interface inherit the default behavior but can override it if needed.
They help avoid the need for utility classes (like the 'XYZUtils' pattern) for common interface-related behavior.
commonMistakes: Assuming default methods are `public` and `abstract` like regular interface methods; they are public but are concrete (have an implementation).
Trying to declare a default method as `final` or `static`; the `default` modifier is exclusive.
Creating overly complex default methods instead of keeping them as simple, default behavior.
Forgetting that a class inherits the default method if it doesn't provide an implementation, which can lead to unexpected behavior if the default logic is not well understood.
Causing ambiguity (a compiler error) by implementing two interfaces that have a `default` method with the same signature, without overriding the method in the class to resolve the conflict.
